title: Wizard-of-Oz Corpus Audit Prototype: AI vs. Human-Authored News Article Accessibility Structure

actors.description: A screen-reader-proficient blind or low-vision adult who regularly reads online news using assistive technology (NVDA or JAWS on Windows, or VoiceOver on macOS); recruited to navigate a curated set of article pages and verbalize navigation decisions while the audit pipeline surfaces structural metadata in a side panel visible only to the wizard.

A researcher seated at a second workstation who monitors the live DOM audit dashboard, manually triggers or suppresses audit overlay annotations on the participant-facing page, and decides in real time which structural divergence flags to surface or hold back based on the participant's current navigation position.

A researcher seated in the same room as the participant who reads task prompts aloud, manages the session timer, and probes think-aloud verbalization without revealing the audit system's back-end logic.

A silent note-taker positioned behind the participant who records timestamped behavioral observations (e.g., hesitation, backtracking with screen reader, repeated heading-list invocations) onto a pre-printed observation log sheet keyed to article IDs.

context.setting: A quiet usability lab room with two workstations separated by a partition: the participant workstation faces away from the wizard workstation. The participant workstation has external speakers for screen-reader audio output. The wizard workstation has a mirrored view of the participant's browser via a local network screen-share (no audio delay). The room is recorded by a fixed overhead camera capturing both screens.
context.device: Participant workstation: Dell laptop running Windows 11, Chrome 124 at 1920x1080, NVDA 2024.1 with default eSpeak NG voice at speed 60 wpm. Wizard workstation: identical Dell laptop running a localhost Node.js audit dashboard (Puppeteer-based DOM parser) at 1920x1080.
context.session_structure: 0–10 min: facilitator reads consent form and orientation script; participant completes warm-up navigation task on a neutral article not in the stimulus set to calibrate screen-reader speed and confirm think-aloud protocol. 10–15 min: facilitator explains the cover story ('We are evaluating a news reading interface') without revealing the AI/human authorship dimension. 15–65 min: five article navigation tasks, each 10 minutes maximum, alternating between AI-generated and human-authored articles in a counterbalanced ABBA-A order; facilitator reads each task prompt and starts a visible countdown timer; wizard operates audit controls throughout. 65–75 min: facilitator administers a 7-item post-task self-report questionnaire on perceived navigation difficulty per article. 75–90 min: semi-structured debrief interview; facilitator reveals the AI/human comparison dimension and probes participant's retrospective attributions.

task_flow.participant_action: Participant opens a pre-loaded article URL in Chrome (article is already rendered; no page load wait required) and is asked by the facilitator to 'navigate to the section that tells you what the article's main argument is, using whatever screen-reader strategy you normally use, and tell us when you have found it.'
task_flow.system_response: The article page renders with its native DOM structure intact. The wizard's audit dashboard simultaneously displays a parsed heading tree (h1–h6 depth counts), a landmark map (header, nav, main, aside, footer presence/absence), and an ARIA violations list (e.g., missing role='region' labels, skipped heading levels) generated by a Puppeteer + axe-core 4.9 scan run at page load. No overlay is shown to the participant at this step.
wizard_decisions: Decide whether the axe-core scan completed cleanly or threw a timeout/parse error requiring a manual re-scan before the participant reaches the first heading.

Decide whether the heading tree displayed on the dashboard matches what NVDA will actually announce (e.g., detect if CSS display:none hides headings from visual but not NVDA) and flag discrepancy for the observer log.
Decide whether to note the participant's initial navigation strategy (heading list keystroke H vs. landmarks keystroke D vs. linear read) as the baseline strategy variable for this article.

task_flow.participant_action: Participant uses NVDA's heading navigation (H key) to move through the article's heading structure and verbalizes each heading label as NVDA announces it. If the participant invokes NVDA's Elements List (NVDA+F7) to view all headings at once, they describe what they see in the list.
task_flow.system_response: The wizard's dashboard highlights in red any heading level that is skipped (e.g., h2 followed directly by h4) and in amber any heading whose visible text in the DOM differs from the accessible name announced by NVDA (detectable via axe-core 'accessible-name' rule). The participant's browser shows no change. If the wizard activates the 'inject heading annotation overlay' control (see wizard_controls), a visually-positioned tooltip appears in the participant's browser adjacent to each heading showing its h-level badge.
wizard_decisions: Decide whether to activate the heading annotation overlay for this article based on whether the heading divergence score (number of skipped levels + mislabeled headings) exceeds the pre-defined threshold of ≥2 violations — if below threshold, withhold overlay to preserve ecological validity of the navigation task.

Decide whether a participant pause of >8 seconds at a heading boundary represents genuine disorientation caused by structural anomaly or simply slow reading pace, and record the judgment with a timestamp in the wizard log.

Decide whether the participant's verbalized heading label matches or diverges from the axe-core accessible name, flagging mismatches as a live data point indicating NVDA rendering divergence from DOM source.

task_flow.participant_action: Participant is asked by the facilitator to 'jump to the comments section or any reader-response area at the bottom of the article using the fastest method available to you' and to verbalize if they cannot locate it within 60 seconds.
task_flow.system_response: The wizard's dashboard displays the article's full landmark structure: presence or absence of a contentinfo or complementary landmark wrapping the comments section, and whether any nav or aside elements appear after the main content block. If the participant cannot locate the target within 45 seconds, the wizard may activate the 'inject landmark breadcrumb' control to add an ARIA-label to the comments container visible only in the accessibility tree (not visually), which NVDA will then announce on the next D-key landmark press.
wizard_decisions: Decide at the 45-second mark whether participant failure to locate comments is attributable to absent landmark markup (confirmed by dashboard showing no contentinfo or complementary landmark after main) versus navigation strategy error (participant skipping D-key landmarks entirely), and record the causal attribution.

Decide whether activating the 'inject landmark breadcrumb' control would contaminate the ecological validity of this article's navigation measurement, and if so, withhold and instead allow the task to time out and log as a navigation failure.

Decide whether an article with zero landmarks after main should be flagged immediately in the corpus audit log as a structural severity-3 violation (complete absence of post-content landmarks) versus severity-2 (landmarks present but mislabeled).

task_flow.participant_action: Participant is given a specific factual question about the article content (e.g., 'What year does the article say the policy was enacted?') and asked to locate the answer using any navigation strategy, verbalizing their search path. The facilitator starts a 3-minute countdown timer visible to the observer but not the participant.
task_flow.system_response: The wizard's dashboard activates a 'search navigation trace' view that logs every NVDA focus-change event (captured via a background accessibility event listener script injected at page load) and maps each event to a DOM node with its heading ancestry path. This creates a real-time breadcrumb trail showing exactly which structural nodes the participant is traversing. No change is presented to the participant's browser unless the wizard activates the 'inject search landmark' control.
wizard_decisions: Decide whether the participant's navigation path is heading-driven, landmark-driven, or linear-read, and annotate the trace log with this classification in real time for later inter-rater reliability coding.

Decide whether a failure to find the answer within 3 minutes should be logged as a navigation structure failure (if the answer is under a heading that axe-core flagged as inaccessible) versus a content comprehension failure (if the answer is under a correctly structured heading the participant passed over), distinguishing these two causes in the wizard log.

Decide whether to trigger the 'inject search landmark' control if the target information is inside a div with no heading ancestry and no landmark wrapper — a structural gap that is unambiguously the article's fault rather than the participant's navigation strategy.

task_flow.participant_action: After completing all five articles, participant fills out a 7-item paper questionnaire rating navigation difficulty, heading predictability, and landmark usefulness for each article on a 5-point Likert scale (1 = very easy / very predictable / very useful; 5 = very difficult / unpredictable / useless). Participant then answers three open-ended questions about which articles felt structurally different and why.
task_flow.system_response: No system response during questionnaire. The wizard uses this time to finalize the audit log: marking each article's final violation counts, recording all wizard decisions made during that article's task steps, and exporting the Puppeteer/axe-core JSON report for each article to a timestamped file named {article_id}_{session_id}_audit.json.
wizard_decisions: Decide whether any wizard interventions during the session (overlay injections, landmark injections) need to be flagged as confounds in the audit log that will require exclusion or sensitivity analysis in the post-session data merge.
Decide whether the session-level audit export is complete and all five article DOM snapshots were captured without Puppeteer render failures before closing the dashboard.

wizard_controls.triggers: Wizard activates when the article's heading divergence score (skipped levels + mislabeled accessible names) is ≥2 AND the participant has paused at a heading boundary for >8 seconds without advancing, indicating structural confusion rather than reading pace.
wizard_controls.effect: A JavaScript snippet pre-loaded in the participant's browser (but dormant until triggered via a WebSocket message from the wizard dashboard) inserts visually-positioned badge elements adjacent to each heading showing its h-level (e.g., a small grey '[H2]' badge). The badges are added to the live DOM and are announced by NVDA as part of the heading text, so NVDA reads 'H2: Policy Background [H2]'. This allows the researcher to observe whether structural labeling changes navigation efficiency on the next heading-list invocation.

wizard_controls.triggers: Wizard activates when the participant has been attempting to locate a specific page section (comments, related articles, author bio) for >45 seconds AND the audit dashboard confirms the target section has no landmark role or ARIA-label in its DOM wrapper.
wizard_controls.effect: A WebSocket-triggered JavaScript snippet adds role='region' and aria-label='Comments section' (or the appropriate section name) to the target container's outermost div in the live DOM without altering visual rendering. NVDA will announce the new landmark on the participant's next D-key landmark navigation press. The wizard logs the exact timestamp of injection and the participant's next navigation event to measure whether the injected landmark resolves the blockage within two D-key presses.

wizard_controls.triggers: Wizard activates if the Puppeteer/axe-core background process begins consuming >80% CPU (visible in the Node.js process monitor in the dashboard's status bar), which would introduce audio lag in NVDA output on the shared machine if the audit and browser are co-hosted — a known risk on the lab's current hardware configuration.
wizard_controls.effect: Pauses the continuous DOM re-scan loop (which runs every 15 seconds to catch dynamic content injection) and switches the dashboard to a static snapshot mode using the last completed scan. Wizard notes the freeze timestamp and duration in the session log. The participant's browser and NVDA are unaffected. The wizard manually resumes the scan loop at the next task-step boundary (i.e., between articles) to avoid mid-task CPU spikes.

wizard_controls.triggers: Wizard activates at any point during any task step when making a causal attribution (navigation failure due to structure vs. strategy) or recording a discrepancy between the axe-core report and NVDA's actual announcement behavior.
wizard_controls.effect: Appends a timestamped free-text entry to the wizard_judgment_log.jsonl file with fields: {session_id, article_id, step, timestamp_ms, judgment_type, judgment_text}. This log is the primary source for inter-rater reliability coding in post-session analysis and must be complete before the session debrief begins.

observable_signals.signal: NVDA focus-change event trace mapped to DOM node heading ancestry path for each article navigation task
observable_signals.analysis_plan: The accessibility event listener script (injected at page load via a browser extension set to always-on for the lab Chrome profile) writes each focus-change event to a local log file: {timestamp_ms, node_tag, node_role, node_aria_label, heading_ancestor_chain}. After the session, each article's event log is merged with the axe-core audit JSON for that article. For each navigation task, the analyst computes: (a) number of focus events before target node reached, (b) proportion of events landing on structurally flagged nodes (nodes inside axe-core violation contexts), and (c) whether task success/failure aligns with the presence of a severity-3 structural violation on the path to the target. Articles are then split into AI-authored vs. human-authored groups and these three metrics are compared using a two-sample permutation test (n per group determined by corpus sampling), with effect sizes reported as Cohen's d. Wizard-intervened tasks (overlay or landmark injections) are excluded from primary analysis and treated in a sensitivity analysis.

observable_signals.signal: Think-aloud verbalization at heading and landmark boundaries, specifically instances where participant explicitly names a navigation obstacle or expresses uncertainty about where they are in the article
observable_signals.analysis_plan: Session audio is transcribed using automated speech-to-text (Whisper large-v3) and manually corrected against the screen recording. Two independent coders apply a binary code to each 10-second segment: 0 = no navigation obstacle verbalized, 1 = navigation obstacle verbalized. Codes are applied blind to article authorship condition. Inter-rater reliability is computed as Cohen's kappa; segments with disagreement are resolved by a third coder. The proportion of obstacle-verbalization segments per article is then compared between AI-authored and human-authored article groups using a Wilcoxon signed-rank test (paired by outlet, since both conditions include articles from the same outlets). This operationalizes 'which structural dimensions degrade navigation' as the subset of axe-core violation types that co-occur with obstacle verbalizations at a rate above the session-level base rate.

observable_signals.signal: Post-task Likert ratings of navigation difficulty, heading predictability, and landmark usefulness for each of the five articles
observable_signals.analysis_plan: The 7-item paper questionnaire is scored item-by-item. Three subscale scores are computed: navigation difficulty (2 items, mean), heading predictability (3 items, mean), landmark usefulness (2 items, mean). Article-level subscale scores are merged with that article's axe-core violation count (total violations, heading-specific violations, landmark-specific violations) and with the navigation trace metrics from the log_events signal. Spearman rank correlations are computed between each subscale and each violation-count dimension to identify which structural metric type (heading vs. landmark vs. ARIA label) most strongly covaries with subjective navigation difficulty. Given the small within-session sample (5 articles), these correlations are treated as pilot estimates for power analysis for a subsequent full-corpus session series, not as inferential results.

observable_signals.signal: Task completion time per article navigation task (time from facilitator's 'go' cue to participant's verbal declaration of having found the target, or to timeout at 3 or 10 minutes)
observable_signals.analysis_plan: The observer records start and end timestamps to the second on the pre-printed observation log. Times are entered into a session spreadsheet after the session. For each article, completion time is coded as: completed-within-time (exact seconds), timed-out (coded as the maximum allowed duration), or abandoned (participant explicitly gave up before timeout, coded separately). Completion times are compared between AI-authored and human-authored articles within-session using the navigation trace data as a mediator: the analyst tests whether the AI/human difference in completion time (if any) is reduced when controlling for article-level axe-core violation count, operationalizing the claim that structural violations (not authorship per se) drive navigation time differences.

observable_signals.signal: Observer-recorded behavioral markers: number of times participant invokes NVDA Elements List (NVDA+F7) per article, number of heading-backward navigations (Shift+H), and number of explicit verbal requests for help per article
observable_signals.analysis_plan: The observer's pre-printed log has one row per article with three tally columns: Elements List invocations, backward heading navigations, help requests. After the session, tallies are digitized and treated as behavioral proxies for disorientation. Elements List invocations and backward heading navigations are summed into a 'disorientation index' per article. This index is correlated with the article's axe-core heading-structure violation count (skipped levels + reversed nesting) using Spearman rho across the five articles, and the direction and magnitude of the correlation is reported as a formative finding informing which heading violation types to prioritize in the full corpus audit pipeline.

failure_cases.scenario: The Puppeteer/axe-core DOM scan captures a static snapshot of the article's initial HTML but the article uses client-side JavaScript to inject the main body content (common in React/Next.js-built news sites), so the audit dashboard shows a nearly empty heading tree and zero landmarks even though NVDA correctly reads the fully-rendered DOM that Chrome has already hydrated.
failure_cases.expected_behavior: The wizard should detect the mismatch immediately because the dashboard heading tree will show only 1–2 headings while the participant is clearly navigating through 8–10 headings announced by NVDA. The wizard log should note this as a static-vs-rendered DOM divergence, not a structural violation in the article.
failure_cases.wizard_fallback: Wizard activates the 'freeze_audit_dashboard' control to stop the re-scan loop (which would keep returning the empty static snapshot), then manually triggers a deferred Puppeteer scan using the '--wait-for-selector=article' flag option exposed as a manual re-scan button on the dashboard. If the re-scan succeeds within 30 seconds, the wizard resumes normal operation. If it fails (e.g., the article selector is non-standard), the wizard annotates the article in the wizard_judgment_log as 'audit-unavailable: JS-rendered content' and the session continues without audit overlay capability for that article; the event trace and think-aloud signals remain fully operative.

failure_cases.scenario: NVDA announces heading levels that differ from the actual DOM h-tag level because the article uses CSS-only heading styling (a div with role='heading' and aria-level='2' that axe-core scores as conformant) but a second article uses presentational h3 tags inside a main landmark that axe-core also scores as conformant — making the axe-core violation count identical for two articles that NVDA navigates very differently, collapsing a meaningful structural difference into the same audit score.
failure_cases.expected_behavior: The wizard should detect this when the dashboard shows zero violations for an article but the participant's navigation trace shows repeated backward heading navigations (Shift+H) indicating unexpected heading order, which contradicts the clean audit score. This is the most likely way the audit pipeline produces false-negative structural assessments.
failure_cases.wizard_fallback: Wizard activates the 'log_wizard_judgment' control with judgment_type='axe-false-negative' and records the specific node path where the discrepancy occurs (e.g., 'div[role=heading][aria-level=2] inside article, visually appears as subheading of preceding h3 but NVDA reads at same level'). The wizard also notes the participant's behavioral response. After the session, these logged false-negatives are used to extend the audit pipeline with a supplementary NVDA-simulation check (using the Accessibility Tree API via Chrome DevTools Protocol) that computes the announced heading level independently of axe-core's DOM-based scoring, specifically to catch aria-level mismatches of this type.

failure_cases.scenario: Participant's NVDA speech output is fast enough (>120 wpm) that the observer cannot reliably tally Elements List invocations and backward heading navigations by ear alone, leading to undercounting of disorientation behavioral markers for fast-navigation participants.
failure_cases.expected_behavior: The observer's tally counts will be systematically lower for fast-navigation participants, making the disorientation index unreliable as a between-article comparison if the participant's speed varies by article.
failure_cases.wizard_fallback: The screen recording (captured via OBS on the participant workstation at 30fps) includes NVDA's audio output. After the session, a research assistant reviews the screen recording at 0.5x speed and re-tallies all NVDA keystroke events using the on-screen NVDA speech viewer (a floating NVDA window showing the last 5 announced strings, enabled by default in the lab Chrome profile setup). The observer's real-time counts are treated as backup only; the screen-recording-derived counts are used as the primary disorientation index source.

materials_needed: Ten pre-selected article URLs: five AI-generated articles and five human-authored articles from the same three news outlets known to use LLM pipelines (outlets confirmed via public editorial disclosure statements); articles matched on approximate word count (800–1200 words) and topic category (domestic policy) to control for content-driven structural variation.

A pre-built localhost Node.js audit dashboard application (Puppeteer 22 + axe-core 4.9) that accepts an article URL, renders it in a headless Chromium instance with a 5-second JavaScript execution wait, extracts the full heading tree (tag, depth, accessible name, visible text, axe-core violation flags), extracts all ARIA landmark roles with their labels, and displays results in a split-pane dashboard with a red/amber/green violation severity color scheme.

A Chrome browser extension (Manifest V3, pre-installed in the lab Chrome profile) that: (a) injects the accessibility event listener script on every page load to write NVDA focus-change events to a local file via a Native Messaging host, (b) listens on a local WebSocket port (ws://localhost:9001) for wizard trigger messages to activate the heading annotation overlay and landmark breadcrumb injection scripts, and (c) displays a discreet status indicator in the Chrome toolbar showing WebSocket connection state.

Native Messaging host application (Python 3.11 script packaged as a Windows executable) that receives accessibility events from the Chrome extension and writes them to {session_id}_{article_id}_events.jsonl in real time.

Pre-printed facilitator script: verbatim text for the consent reading, cover story, warm-up task instruction, five task-step prompts (one per article), and debrief reveal script. All prompts are written at a 7th-grade reading level and reviewed by a screen-reader user consultant before the first session.

Pre-printed observer log sheet: one A4 sheet per session with five article rows, each row containing tally boxes for Elements List invocations, backward heading navigations, and help requests, plus a timestamp column and a free-text notes column.

Seven-item Likert questionnaire (paper, 14-point font for low-vision accessibility): items 1–2 measure navigation difficulty (1=very easy, 5=very difficult), items 3–5 measure heading predictability (1=very predictable, 5=very unpredictable), items 6–7 measure landmark usefulness (1=very useful, 5=not useful at all). Each item is labeled with the article number (1–5) so participants rate each article separately on a single sheet.

Three open-ended debrief questions printed on a separate sheet, administered verbally by the facilitator with participant responses audio-recorded: (Q1) 'Were there any articles where you felt lost or unsure of where you were — and if so, what made that happen?', (Q2) 'Did any articles feel more predictably organized than others? What was different?', (Q3) 'Is there anything about how these articles were structured that you would want the editor to fix?'

OBS Studio 30.2 configured to simultaneously capture: (a) the participant workstation screen at 1920x1080 30fps with NVDA audio output mixed in, and (b) the overhead room camera feed at 720p 30fps. Both recordings are saved to separate files with the session_id as filename prefix.

A counterbalancing schedule spreadsheet pre-computed for 12 planned sessions (to cover all ABBA-A permutations across the five articles with outlet and authorship-condition balanced across sessions), so each session can be assigned a row from the schedule without recalculation.

Wizard decision log template (wizard_judgment_log.jsonl): a pre-created empty JSONL file with schema documentation comment at line 1, opened in a text editor on the wizard workstation at session start.

NVDA 2024.1 installer and the NVDA Speech Viewer add-on, both verified compatible with Windows 11 and Chrome 124 before each session day using a 5-minute pre-session hardware check protocol documented in a one-page setup checklist.
